front: Which OSI layers matter most in practice, and what does each one give you?
back: The full 7-layer model is mostly theoretical. The layers engineers actually reason about: L2 (Data Link) — Ethernet frames, MAC addresses, ARP, switches operate here. L3 (Network) — IP addresses, routing, IP fragmentation, ICMP. L4 (Transport) — TCP/UDP, port numbers, multiplexing connections over a single IP. L5–6 (Session/Presentation) — largely absorbed by TLS in practice. L7 (Application) — HTTP, DNS, gRPC, your protocol. Firewalls operate at L3/L4 (stateless) or L7 (application-aware). Load balancers split between L4 (fast, no TLS termination) and L7 (can route by HTTP host/path, terminates TLS). Knowing the layer tells you where a tool can intervene and what it can see.
senior_nuance: The TCP/IP model (4 layers: Link, Internet, Transport, Application) is what real implementations follow. Engineers say 'L7 load balancer' using OSI numbering but implement TCP/IP. When debugging, knowing the layer helps: tcpdump sees L2/L3/L4, strace sees L7 from the application's perspective, eBPF can see any layer.
quote_to_say: OSI is a vocabulary, not an implementation guide.

front: TCP vs UDP: when do you choose each, and what does TCP's reliability actually cost you?
back: TCP guarantees: ordered delivery, no duplicates, no loss (retransmissions), flow control (receiver window), congestion control. UDP guarantees: nothing beyond best-effort delivery and checksum integrity. TCP costs: (1) 1 RTT minimum to establish (3-way handshake), (2) head-of-line blocking — a lost packet stalls all subsequent data until retransmit, (3) connection state on both sides, (4) congestion control adds latency during loss events. UDP is preferred when: low latency matters more than reliability (DNS, real-time gaming, VoIP), the application has its own reliability layer (QUIC), or multicast/broadcast is needed (TCP is unicast only). Reliability built on UDP (QUIC, WebRTC) can outperform TCP by avoiding HoL blocking at the transport layer.
senior_nuance: TCP is not always reliable in the way applications think — the kernel ACKs data reaching the socket buffer, not data read by the application. A server crash after ACKing but before processing data is invisible to TCP. Applications needing true end-to-end guarantees use application-level ACKs (e.g., Kafka offset commit, database transactions).
quote_to_say: TCP is reliable to the kernel buffer, not to your application logic.

front: Walk through the TCP 3-way handshake and explain TIME_WAIT. Why does SO_REUSEADDR exist?
back: Handshake: (1) Client sends SYN (seq=x). (2) Server responds SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1). (3) Client sends ACK (ack=y+1). Connection is established. The active closer enters TIME_WAIT for 2×MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime, typically 2×60s = 2 min) after sending the final ACK. TIME_WAIT ensures: (a) the final ACK reaches the server (if lost, server retransmits FIN), (b) delayed packets from the old connection are not mistaken for data in a new connection on the same 4-tuple. After restart, the OS refuses to bind to a port that has TIME_WAIT entries. SO_REUSEADDR bypasses this restriction for servers by allowing bind to a port with TIME_WAIT sockets — safe because the kernel still prevents ambiguous packet delivery. tcp_tw_reuse is the sysctl equivalent for outbound connections.
senior_nuance: TIME_WAIT exhaustion is a real production problem for clients making many short connections (e.g., HTTP/1.0 to a backend). Symptoms: connect() returns EADDRNOTAVAIL when ephemeral ports are exhausted. Fixes: connection pooling, keepalive, increasing the ephemeral port range (ip_local_port_range), or enabling tcp_tw_reuse. High TIME_WAIT count on a server is a canary for HTTP/1.0 or connection-per-request patterns.
quote_to_say: TIME_WAIT is not a bug — it is the guarantee that old packets cannot corrupt new connections.

front: Explain TCP slow start, and how do cubic and BBR differ in their approach to congestion?
back: Slow start: at connection open, cwnd (congestion window) = 1 MSS (max segment size). Each ACK doubles cwnd (exponential growth) until ssthresh. Above ssthresh, AIMD (additive increase / multiplicative decrease) kicks in: +1 MSS per RTT until loss, then halve cwnd. Cubic (Linux default): loss-based, uses a cubic function to grow cwnd, targeting the last known safe window size. Cubic backs off significantly on loss, causing throughput sawtooth. BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and RTT): model-based, not loss-based. BBR estimates the bottleneck bandwidth and RTT independently, maintains cwnd at bandwidth×RTT (BDP), and probes for more bandwidth in cycles. BBR achieves higher throughput on lossy links (e.g., wireless, intercontinental) because packet loss ≠ congestion in its model. Downside: BBR can be unfair to cubic flows sharing the same bottleneck.
senior_nuance: On high-bandwidth long-distance links (e.g., 10Gbps cross-continent), Cubic never fills the pipe because slow start is too conservative and loss signals come too late. BBR was developed at Google for exactly this use case. However BBR v1 has known unfairness issues; BBR v2/v3 are still being standardized. Production CDNs typically run BBR on edge-to-client paths.
quote_to_say: Cubic backs off on loss; BBR never confuses loss with congestion.

front: Walk through the TLS 1.3 handshake. How does it achieve 1-RTT, and what does 0-RTT cost?
back: TLS 1.3 handshake: (1) Client sends ClientHello with supported cipher suites, key share (Diffie-Hellman public key), and session ticket if resuming. (2) Server responds in one message: ServerHello (chosen cipher + DH public key), EncryptedExtensions, Certificate, CertificateVerify (signature proving possession of private key), Finished (HMAC over transcript). Both sides derive shared secrets from DH exchange immediately. (3) Client sends Finished. First application data can be sent by client alongside its Finished — 1 RTT total (vs TLS 1.2's 2 RTT). Forward secrecy is mandatory: ephemeral DH keys mean past traffic cannot be decrypted even if the server's long-term key is later compromised. 0-RTT (early data): client sends data in the first flight using a pre-shared key from the previous session. Limitation: 0-RTT data has no replay protection — a middlebox can replay the early data. Only idempotent requests (GET) should use 0-RTT.
senior_nuance: TLS 1.3 removed static RSA key exchange (no forward secrecy), CBC mode ciphers, MD5/SHA1, and export-grade crypto. Approved cipher suites are all AEAD (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305). The ESNI/ECH extension (Encrypted Client Hello) hides the SNI hostname from network observers — required for true metadata privacy. Resumption uses PSK (pre-shared key) derived from the previous session, not the server's long-term key.
quote_to_say: TLS 1.3 exchanges keys before the server even speaks — that is the 1-RTT secret.

front: Compare HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3. What problem does each version solve?
back: HTTP/1.1: one request at a time per connection (pipelining exists but is rarely used due to HoL blocking). Workaround: open 6 connections per origin, leading to connection proliferation. HTTP/2: multiplexes multiple streams over a single TCP connection using binary frames with stream IDs. Eliminates per-request connection overhead. Still suffers TCP-level HoL blocking: a single lost TCP packet stalls all HTTP/2 streams until retransmit. Also adds server push, header compression (HPACK). HTTP/3: replaces TCP with QUIC (built on UDP). QUIC implements per-stream loss recovery — a lost packet only stalls the affected stream, not others. Also encrypts transport metadata, enables 0-RTT connection resumption, and improves connection migration (useful for mobile, where IP changes). QUIC's connection ID allows seamless IP/port changes without reconnection.
senior_nuance: HTTP/2 server push was deprecated in Chrome and is being removed from the spec — implementations never got the heuristics right, causing over-pushing. HTTP/2 header compression (HPACK) uses a dynamic table shared across streams; QPACK (HTTP/3) solves the ordering dependency that made HPACK incompatible with out-of-order QUIC delivery.
quote_to_say: HTTP/2 multiplexes over TCP; HTTP/3 multiplexes over QUIC — one has HoL blocking, the other does not.

front: What is QUIC and why does it exist in userspace rather than as a new kernel transport protocol?
back: QUIC is a multiplexed, encrypted transport protocol built on UDP, originally developed by Google, now standardized as RFC 9000. It implements: stream multiplexing (independent loss recovery per stream), mandatory TLS 1.3 encryption of all payload and most headers, 0-RTT connection resumption, and connection migration via opaque connection IDs (not tied to IP:port). It runs in userspace because: (1) kernel transport protocols take years to deploy globally — middleboxes (firewalls, NATs) often block or corrupt unknown protocols, but UDP port 443 is already open, (2) userspace allows rapid iteration (Google shipped QUIC at scale before IETF standardization), (3) different applications can ship different QUIC versions without kernel updates. Cost: no kernel offloads (GRO, GSO) for QUIC yet, leading to higher CPU usage vs TCP at equivalent throughput.
senior_nuance: QUIC has higher per-packet CPU cost than TCP because encryption is mandatory and kernel offloads are immature. Facebook (Meta) measured QUIC using 2× the CPU of HTTP/2+TLS for equivalent throughput. The tradeoff is acceptable for mobile/lossy paths where QUIC's latency advantage dominates, but may not be worth it on fast wired networks. eBPF-based QUIC acceleration (AF_XDP) is an active research area.
quote_to_say: QUIC lives in userspace because ossified middleboxes made kernel deployment impossible.

front: Walk through a full DNS resolution from browser to authoritative nameserver. What is the role of caching at each level?
back: Resolution path for first lookup of 'api.example.com': (1) Browser DNS cache (respects TTL, typically seconds to minutes). (2) OS resolver cache (/etc/nsswitch.conf, /etc/hosts first, then stub resolver). (3) Recursive resolver (usually ISP or 8.8.8.8) — checks its cache; if miss, starts from root. (4) Root nameserver returns NS records for .com TLD nameservers. (5) TLD nameserver (.com) returns NS records for example.com's authoritative nameservers. (6) Authoritative nameserver for example.com returns the A/AAAA record for api.example.com. The recursive resolver caches each step according to its TTL. TTL for A records is typically 60–300s for CDN/dynamic IPs, up to 86400s for stable servers. Negative cache (NXDOMAIN) is also cached — short-TTL NXDOMAIN causes repeated queries.
senior_nuance: Low TTL (60s) enables fast failover/blue-green at the cost of more queries and cache misses. High TTL (3600s+) reduces query load and latency but means DNS changes propagate slowly. DNSSEC adds cryptographic signatures to prevent cache poisoning (Kaminsky attack). DoH (DNS over HTTPS) and DoT (DNS over TLS) encrypt queries to prevent ISP snooping and tampering — browsers now default to DoH with fallback.
quote_to_say: DNS TTL is your failover latency — low TTL is fast recovery, high TTL is caching efficiency.

front: How does NAT work, and what problems does it create for peer-to-peer and server applications?
back: NAT (Network Address Translation) rewrites IP headers and TCP/UDP port fields as packets pass through a gateway, mapping many private (RFC 1918) addresses to one or a few public IPs. The NAT device maintains a connection tracking table: (private IP, private port) ↔ (public IP, public port). Packets returning from the public internet are rewritten back to the private destination. Problems: (1) Inbound connections are impossible without port forwarding — the NAT has no entry for unsolicited traffic. P2P apps use STUN (Session Traversal Utilities for NAT) or TURN (relay) to punch holes. (2) NAT breaks IP-level end-to-end connectivity (IPsec ESP is problematic, some UDP-based protocols embed IP:port in payload). (3) CGNAT (carrier-grade NAT) stacks two NAT layers, breaking even more protocols and making tracing traffic to a customer impossible without flow logging. (4) IPv6 makes NAT unnecessary — every device gets a globally routable address.
senior_nuance: Kubernetes services use kube-proxy (iptables/ipvs NAT rules) to map ClusterIP to pod IPs — every service access is a NAT translation. High-traffic clusters see iptables rule chains becoming a performance bottleneck at O(n rules) per packet. eBPF-based alternatives (Cilium with eBPF kube-proxy replacement) do O(1) lookups via BPF maps instead.
quote_to_say: NAT solved IPv4 exhaustion by breaking the end-to-end principle.

front: What is TCP keepalive, and why is it insufficient for detecting dead peers in application protocols?
back: TCP keepalive sends empty probe segments on idle connections after tcp_keepalive_time (default 7200s on Linux), retrying tcp_keepalive_probes times with tcp_keepalive_intvl interval. If no response, the kernel closes the connection with ETIMEDOUT. Problems with relying on OS keepalive: (1) defaults are 2 hours — application may block for hours on a dead connection before OS acts, (2) NAT state tables often expire idle entries in minutes, making the underlying transport invisible to keepalive, (3) half-open connections (remote host crashed, not cleanly closed) may not be detected at all if the path is asymmetric. Best practice: implement application-level heartbeats with timeouts (e.g., 10s ping/pong in WebSocket, HTTP keepalive on HTTP/1.1 idle timeout). Database connection pools always implement their own liveness checks.
senior_nuance: gRPC uses HTTP/2 PING frames as application-level keepalive (KEEPALIVE_TIME, KEEPALIVE_TIMEOUT options). Cloud load balancers (AWS ALB, GCP) silently terminate idle connections after a timeout (typically 60s) without sending RST — this causes 'read: connection reset by peer' errors in application pools that don't use keepalive. Always configure connection pool max-idle-time < load balancer idle timeout.
quote_to_say: OS keepalive is 2 hours by default; your load balancer timeout is 60 seconds — set application heartbeats.

front: Consistent 40–200ms latency on small TCP writes, no network congestion. Likely cause?
back: Nagle's algorithm + delayed ACK interaction. Nagle buffers small writes until the previous unACKed segment is acknowledged, to reduce small-packet flood. Delayed ACK waits up to 200ms before sending an ACK, hoping to piggyback it on return data. Together: (1) client writes small chunk, sends it. (2) Server receives, starts delayed-ACK timer. (3) Client has more data, but Nagle holds it waiting for ACK. (4) 200ms later, server's delayed-ACK fires. (5) Client unblocks and sends next chunk. Fix: set TCP_NODELAY on the sending socket to disable Nagle. Any RPC library doing small-message request/response should always set TCP_NODELAY.
senior_nuance: Invisible at the TCP layer — wireshark shows both sides behaving per spec. Only shows in p99 latency and only if you know to correlate message size with latency spikes. Redis, memcached, and most databases disable Nagle. Go's net package disables Nagle by default. Node.js does NOT disable it by default — production Node TCP servers should call socket.setNoDelay(true).
quote_to_say: Nagle plus delayed ACK is the silent latency killer for small-RPC workloads.

front: How does anycast routing work, and why do CDNs and DNS providers use it?
back: Anycast assigns the same IP address to multiple servers in different geographic locations. BGP routing naturally delivers packets to the topologically nearest node advertising that prefix. The sender has no knowledge of which server will respond — the network makes the routing decision. Benefits: (1) automatic geographic load distribution, (2) DDoS absorption — attack traffic hits the nearest PoP and is absorbed locally rather than tunneled to a central origin, (3) reduced latency — packets travel to the closest PoP, (4) no single point of failure — if one PoP fails, BGP converges to the next nearest. Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1 and Google's 8.8.8.8 are anycast. TCP is theoretically fragile with anycast (route changes mid-connection send packets to a different server), but in practice CDNs use anycast for TCP because route changes are rare and gracefully handled by connection migration.
senior_nuance: UDP-based protocols (DNS, QUIC) are a natural fit for anycast because each packet is independent. TCP anycast works in practice but not in theory — a route flap during a connection resets it. This is another reason QUIC's connection migration (connection ID not tied to IP) pairs well with anycast infrastructure.
quote_to_say: Anycast is the internet routing the packet to the nearest copy of your server automatically.
